message.reasoning_content:
We need to fill the skeleton for arxiv 2101.10878. The inputs are given: catalog_review, catalog_extraction, and the paper source. The paper is "Blue extreme disk-runaway stars with Gaia EDR3". The task is to identify all objects the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way (Galactic potential) and fill candidates accordingly.

Let's analyze the paper. From the abstract: "only three stars ... have past trajectories consistent with an origin in the GC, most notably S5-HVS1 ... All other program stars are shown to be disk runaways ... They include HVS5 and HVS6, which are both gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." Later sections detail. So the paper explicitly identifies some objects as gravitationally unbound. The candidates are the 30 program stars listed in Table 1 (table:vejection). But not all are unbound; many are bound. The paper classifies them based on flag F: C (possible GC origin), D (bona fide disk runaways), R (outer rim). They discuss that HVS5 and HVS6 are unbound, S5-HVS1 is unbound, and others might be hyper-runaway if certain mass model is used (LAMOST HVS1, LAMOST HVS4, etc.) and some are bound (HD 271791, HIP 60350). The criteria for inclusion: any star that the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way. So we need to extract all objects where the paper either states or implies they are gravitationally unbound or possibly unbound, or hyper-runaway candidates with Galactic-unbound status. The paper's Table 1 includes all 30 stars, but the unbound status is assessed from kinematics in Table 3 (kinematics_AS) and discussion. In the text, they say: "They include HVS 5 and HVS 6, which are both gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." And later in the subsection on possible origin in outer rim: "HVS 6 is unbound to the Milky Way while B481 and HVS 7 might be." Also in the discussion of disk origin: "Although slightly less extreme ... HVS 5 is certainly one of the most interesting targets because it is, on the one hand, clearly gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way". For other stars, they mention that LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are hyper-runaway stars (meaning unbound? The text: "LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are two further examples for such so-called hyper-runaway stars." The term "hyper-runaway star" was coined for stars gravitationally unbound but not from GC. So these are likely unbound. Then they say: "Interestingly, the object for which the hyper-runaway class was originally coined, HD 271791, no longer qualifies as member because it is clearly bound to the Milky Way. The same applies to HIP 60350. Whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So those are possibly unbound depending on model. Also the outer-rim group includes B481 and HVS 7 which might be unbound. And S5-HVS1 is unbound. So we need to extract all 30 objects from the table, and in inclusion_assessment for each, note if the paper claims unbound, likely unbound, possibly unbound, etc. Then include as candidates if they have any claim of possibly unbound. The paper explicitly labels some as unbound, some as possibly unbound, some as bound. But the task says "include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way, even when there are dozens". So we must include all program stars that are not definitively bound. Which are definitively bound? The paper states HD 271791 is clearly bound, HIP 60350 is bound. Others may be bound or unbound based on model. The paper says "Whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So they are possibly unbound. The other stars may be classified as bona fide disk runaways (D) but still could be unbound? The paper's definition of "hyper-runaway star" is unbound to the Galaxy. So any star that is considered a hyper-runaway candidate or unbound. The text: "All other program stars are shown to be disk runaways with ejection velocities that sharply contrast at least with classical ejection scenarios. They include HVS 5 and HVS 6, which are both gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." So the other disk runaways are not necessarily unbound; they are extreme runaways but may be bound. So we need to check which ones the paper says are unbound or possibly unbound.

Let's read the paper carefully.

Abstract: "They include HVS 5 and HVS 6, which are both gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way."

Section 3.1 (Possible origin in GC): "Only three program stars have galactocentric radii at plane intersection that are smaller than 1 kpc and may thus stem from the GC: S5-HVS1, B576, and B598. ... S5-HVS1 clearly standing out ... It is also the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way." So S5-HVS1 is unbound. For B576 and B598, they don't say unbound; they say possible GC origin, but nature not clear. No claim of unbound.

Section 3.2 (Origin in the Galactic disk): They mention HVS5 is clearly gravitationally unbound. LAMOST HVS1 and LAMOST HVS4 are hyper-runaway stars. Then: "Whether LAMOST HVS2, J0136+2425, and HVS8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So those are possibly unbound. HD 271791 bound, HIP 60350 bound. What about other stars? The text: "The higher precision of Gaia EDR3 also allows us to strengthen our previous argument that the well-studied MS disk-runaway star PG 1610+062 is a serious challenge for classical ejection scenarios." But doesn't say it's unbound. So maybe bound? Not sure.

Section 3.3 (Possible origin in outer rim): "HVS 6 is unbound to the Milky Way while B481 and HVS 7 might be." So B481 and HVS 7 possibly unbound. Also B434, B711 are outer-rim stars, but not claimed unbound. So B481 and HVS 7 possibly unbound.

Thus the candidates that are possibly unbound: S5-HVS1 (unbound), HVS5 (unbound), HVS6 (unbound), LAMOST HVS1 (unbound/hyper-runaway), LAMOST HVS4 (hyper-runaway), LAMOST HVS2 (possibly unbound), J0136+2425 (possibly unbound), HVS8 (possibly unbound), B481 (possibly unbound), HVS7 (possibly unbound). Plus maybe others? The paper doesn't claim other stars are unbound. So we should include those 10.

But note B537 is flagged as D and not discussed as unbound. B598, B576 are GC candidates, no unbound claim. B434, B711 are outer rim but no unbound claim. So only those 10.

The task says exhaustive: include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound. So we'll include these 10. Others in Table 1 are not possibly unbound according to the paper. So we can have candidate_groups_considered for the full program stars, and the candidates extracted.

Now, need to fill the extraction status: should be candidates_found since there are candidates. Summary: summarize the inclusion.

Now method_chain: we need to build a DAG of steps. The paper describes: input catalog (Gaia EDR3 for proper motions, spectrophotometric distances from own analysis + literature, radial velocities from spectra). Sample selection from initial sample of MMT-HVS survey stars and others, cut based on ejection velocity threshold and disk origin. For kinematics: Milky Way mass model I of Irrgang et al. 2013. Solar position and motion: from Schönrich et al. 2010. Then orbit integration backward to disk, Monte Carlo, etc. So steps:

- step-01: input_catalog - Gaia EDR3 for proper motions, plus spectrophotometric distances and radial velocities from own analysis and literature.
- step-02: sample_selection - selection of 30 program stars based on high ejection velocity and disk origin criteria.
- step-03: distance_estimation - spectrophotometric distance estimation (using spectral analysis).
- step-04: radial_velocity_measurement - from spectra (various instruments).
- step-05: velocity_calculation - compute Galactocentric positions and velocities, Galactic rest-frame velocity, etc.
- step-06: solar_position_and_motion - adopt R0, solar motion, etc. from Schönrich et al. 2010.
- step-07: galactic_potential_model - Milky Way mass model I of Irrgang et al. 2013.
- step-08: orbit_integration - backward integration to Galactic plane, Monte Carlo.
- step-09: escape_or_bound_assessment - compute escape velocity, bound probability, etc. and assess unbound status.
- step-10: origin_assessment - derive ejection velocity, plane-crossing radius, classification (GC, disk, outer rim).
- step-11: candidate_classification - classify as hyper-runaway, etc.

But we need to include quality_filter maybe for sample selection? The sample selection step includes quality cuts. So we can have step-02 as sample_selection with details. The distances and radial velocities are done before kinematics. So the dependency: input catalog (Gaia EDR3) -> sample selection, then distance estimation and RV measurement are parallel? Actually they use spectrophotometric distances from own analysis and literature, and radial velocities from spectra. So we can have step-03: distance_estimation (spectrophotometric), step-04: radial_velocity_measurement (spectroscopic). Then velocity_calculation (step-05) depends on those and proper motions from Gaia (input catalog) and solar motion. Solar position step-06 depends on external reference. So step-05 depends on step-03, step-04, step-01, step-06. Step-07: galactic_potential_model. Step-08: orbit_integration depends on step-05, step-07. Step-09 depends on step-08. Step-10 depends on step-08, step-09. Step-11 depends on step-09, step-10.

We need to record solar parameters: R0, z0, v_circ_sun, solar motion U,V,W. The paper says: "Uncertainties in the distance of the Sun to the GC and in its relative motion to the local standard of rest (Schönrich et al. 2010) are treated analogously." So they adopt values from that reference, but not explicitly listed in the paper. We need to check the paper's references to see if they quote numbers. The paper text does not explicitly give R0 etc. It just says "we used the Milky Way mass model I of Irrgang et al. (2013)". That model includes solar position and circular velocity. The Irrgang et al. 2013 model uses R0=8.3 kpc, z0=0, v_circ=240 km/s, and solar peculiar motions from Schönrich et al. 2010: U=11.1, V=12.24, W=7.25 km/s. But since the paper doesn't state these, we should note in summary that solar parameters are not reported, but we can infer from the model. According to skill, if the paper does not state them, keep the step and say that the solar parameters are not reported. But we might be able to source them from the cited reference? The skill says "If the paper does not state them, keep the step and say in summary that the solar parameters are not reported; do not guess values." However, the paper says "Uncertainties in the distance of the Sun to the GC and in its relative motion to the local standard of rest (Schönrich et al. 2010) are treated analogously." So it implies they adopt Schönrich's values, but doesn't list them. So I will note that the solar parameters are not reported in the paper but the model Irrgang 2013 is used. I'll include the step with summary "Solar position and motion parameters are not explicitly listed; the paper adopts the values from Irrgang et al. (2013) and Schönrich et al. (2010)." I'll still include parameters with raw_value from the reference? The skill says: "Record the paper's stated values ... as structured parameters[] entries with raw_value, cleaned value, unit, and source_refs. If the paper does not state them, keep the step and say in summary that the solar parameters are not reported; do not guess values." So I cannot fill parameters from external sources. So I'll keep parameters empty and say "not reported" in summary. Similarly for the potential parameters.

Now candidates: For each of the 10, fill identifiers, inclusion_assessment, core, etc.

We have ecsv files for three tables: vejection (ejection velocity, rp, mass, flag), spectrophotometry (Gaia EDR3 id, atmospheric params, stellar params, distances, etc.), kinematics_AS (positions, velocities, Grf, v_esc, P_b, etc.). So we can extract values from those ECSV tables. Also source refs to the paper text lines where unbound claims are made.

For RA/Dec: The paper doesn't give RA/Dec directly, but Gaia EDR3 identifiers are given, so the source catalog is Gaia EDR3. In coordinate_frames.md, Gaia EDR3 uses ICRS, epoch J2016.0, reference_epoch. So we can set ra and dec to null because the paper does not list them, but we could still fill with the Gaia EDR3 identifiers? The skill says we should fill ra and dec if provided; if not, leave null. There's no RA/Dec table. So ra and dec will be null for all candidates. The identifiers include paper_candidate_id (the object name like "S5-HVS 1"), gaia_source_id from table spectrophotometry (col_002). For S5-HVS1, the Gaia EDR3 identifier from spectrophotometry row 1 is "Gaia EDR3 6513109241989477504". So gaia_source_id should be "Gaia EDR3 6513109241989477504". We'll include that as the machine identifier. The record_id will be "2101.10878:cand-001" etc. We'll number in order of Table 1 (vejection) as they appear, but only those 10 candidates. The table order: 1 S5-HVS1 (cand-001), 5 HVS5 (cand-002), 6 HVS6 (cand-003), 8 LAMOST HVS1 (cand-004), 10 LAMOST HVS2 (cand-005), 13 LAMOST HVS4 (cand-006), 14 J0136+2425 (cand-007), 17 HVS7? Wait, HVS7 is number 17? Table: row 17 is HVS 17? Actually table row: after row 14 J0136+2425, row 15 B733, row 16 B711, row 17 HVS 17, row 18 HVS8. HVS7 is #12, HVS8 is #18. So HVS7 is #12, HVS8 is #18. And B481 is #7. So candidate list: S5-HVS1 (#1), HVS5 (#5), HVS6 (#6), LAMOST HVS1 (#8), LAMOST HVS2 (#10), LAMOST HVS4 (#13), J0136+2425 (#14), HVS7 (#12), B481 (#7), HVS8 (#18). But note B481 is #7, HVS7 #12, HVS8 #18. So I'll order them as they appear: cand-001 S5-HVS1, cand-002 B481, cand-003 HVS5, cand-004 HVS6, cand-005 LAMOST HVS1, cand-006 LAMOST HVS2, cand-007 HVS7, cand-008 LAMOST HVS4, cand-009 J0136+2425, cand-010 HVS8. That covers all mentioned.

Now inclusion_assessment: For each, specify paper_labels (hvs_candidate, hyper_runaway_candidate, etc.), galactic_bound_claim (unbound, likely_unbound, possibly_unbound, escaping, not_reported). According to text:
- S5-HVS1: "the only object that is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way" -> unbound.
- HVS5: "clearly gravitationally unbound" -> unbound.
- HVS6: "unbound to the Milky Way" -> unbound.
- LAMOST HVS1: "LAMOST HVS 1 and LAMOST HVS 4 are two further examples for such so-called hyper-runaway stars." Hyper-runaway is defined as unbound. So -> unbound.
- LAMOST HVS4: same -> unbound.
- LAMOST HVS2: "Whether LAMOST HVS 2 ... are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So possibly unbound.
- J0136+2425: similar -> possibly_unbound.
- HVS8: similar -> possibly_unbound.
- B481: "B481 and HVS 7 might be" unbound -> possibly_unbound.
- HVS7: same -> possibly_unbound.

So galactic_bound_claim: unbound for S5-HVS1, HVS5, HVS6, LAMOST HVS1, LAMOST HVS4; possibly_unbound for others. Escaping? Not used.

Paper_labels: The paper uses "hyper-runaway star" for LAMOST HVS1, LAMOST HVS4, and possibly others; "HVS" for the others; "extreme disk-runaway stars". So for each, we can include: hvs_candidate, hyper_runaway_candidate, etc. We'll set appropriately.

Inclusion_basis: explicit_unbound_text for those with clear unbound statements; for possibly unbound, cited_prior_candidate_reassessed? LAMOST HVS2, J0136+2425, HVS8 are discussed as hyper-runaway dependent on model, so explicit candidate text. B481 and HVS7 are in outer rim discussion with "might be" unbound. So explicit_unbound_text.

Extraction_confidence: high for those with clear statements; medium for those that are possibly unbound and dependent on model. Use medium.

Now fill core:

For each candidate, we fill observed_phase_space: ra, dec null. distance from spectrophotometry table col_012 (d in kpc). parallax? Not given. proper motion ra and dec not directly given; we have from Gaia EDR3 but not in these tables. The kinematics table gives derived velocities. So proper motions not needed. Radial velocity from spectrophotometry col_006 (v_rad) and kinematics table includes it? The kinematics table doesn't include observed radial velocity directly; it uses the radial velocity as input. So we can get radial velocity from spectrophotometry col_006, which gives v_rad in km/s. For S5-HVS1: v_rad = 1017.0 +/-2.7 (col_006 row 1). But careful: that table row 1 corresponds to S5-HVS1? Yes, the #1 in spectrophotometry matches the #1 in vejection (S5-HVS1). So for each candidate with number, we can use the corresponding row in spectrophotometry to get v_rad, distance. For B481 (#7), row 7 in spectrophotometry: v_rad=133.1, distance=46. For HVS5 (#5): v_rad=541.5, distance=37. For HVS6 (#6): v_rad=606.3, distance=52. LAMOST HVS1 (#8): v_rad=612.2, distance=15.7. LAMOST HVS2 (#10): v_rad=341.10, distance=22.24. LAMOST HVS4 (#13): v_rad=332.9, distance=69. J0136+2425 (#14): v_rad=324.3, distance=10.9. HVS7 (#12): v_rad=521.8, distance=48. HVS8 (#18): v_rad=498.9, distance=36.

So each candidate's distance and radial velocity from spectrophotometry.

derived_kinematics: from kinematics_AS table (table 3). Each row has number matching. So for each candidate, we extract galactocentric positions (x,y,z), velocities (v_x,v_y,v_z), galactocentric radius r, total velocity v_Grf, escape velocity difference v_Grf - v_esc, bound probability P_b, and at plane crossing: ejection velocity v_ej, plane-crossing radius r_p, etc. The bound assessment includes escape velocity? The table includes v_Grf - v_esc, and P_b (probability bound). So we can use those for bound_assessment. Also escape_velocity_ratio? Not directly, but we have v_Grf and v_esc difference. The paper defines escape velocity relative to the local escape velocity. The table includes v_Grf - v_esc, and bound probability P_b. So bound_assessment: escape_velocity_ratio could be v_Grf / v_esc, but not given. escape_margin is v_Grf - v_esc. bound_probability is P_b (as fraction of Monte Carlo runs bound). The table gives P_b as percentage in column col_012. So we can convert to fraction. For S5-HVS1: P_b = 0 (col_012 row 30: "0"). So bound_probability 0.0, unbound_probability 1.0. For HVS5: P_b = 0 (row 38). So unbound. For HVS6: P_b = 0. For LAMOST HVS1: P_b = 2, so bound_probability 0.02, unbound_probability 0.98. For LAMOST HVS2: P_b = 66, so bound_probability 0.66, unbound_probability 0.34. For LAMOST HVS4: P_b = 0, so unbound. For J0136+2425: P_b = 40, bound 0.40, unbound 0.60. For B481: P_b = 60, bound 0.60, unbound 0.40. For HVS7: P_b = 42, bound 0.42, unbound 0.58. For HVS8: P_b = 55, bound 0.55, unbound 0.45. So these probabilities reflect the paper's assessment.

Now escape velocity v_esc is not directly given, but v_Grf - v_esc is given. We could compute v_esc from v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc) if we have v_Grf. Yes, v_Grf is given in col_010. So we can derive escape_velocity = v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc) if that column is negative? Actually v_Grf - v_esc: if positive, then v_Grf > v_esc. For S5-HVS1: v_Grf - v_esc = 1080, v_Grf = 1680, so v_esc = 600. For HVS5: v_Grf=652, diff=192, v_esc=460. etc. But the paper does not explicitly tabulate v_esc, so we might not fill escape_velocity unless it's directly available. The skill says to include fields if paper provides them. The table provides v_Grf and v_Grf - v_esc, so we can derive escape_velocity as a derived quantity? The field is for the escape velocity value the paper uses. The paper might not list v_esc explicitly; the table gives only differences. So we may leave escape_velocity null. Alternatively, we can fill it using the derived value with a note. Better to leave null and rely on escape_margin (v_Grf - v_esc) which is directly from the table. And bound probability.

For core.derived_kinematics: galactocentric x,y,z from table col_002,003,004, radius from col_005. v_x,v_y,v_z from col_007,008,009. total_velocity v_Grf from col_010. tangential_velocity not directly, might not need. galactocentric_tangential_velocity? Not given.

For star parameters: photometry? Not provided. spectroscopy: perhaps from table spectrophotometry we have spectrograph, but we can note the spectral type? Not directly. Stellar parameters from spectrophotometry: Teff, log g, mass, radius, log L, age tau_evol. So we can fill stellar_parameters. Abundances not given. Quality flags: the flag F from vejection table (C, D, R). So we can include that as a quality flag.

For orbit: from kinematics_AS we have flight time (col_023), pericenter? They have at plane crossing quantities, not orbit parameters like eccentricity. So orbit can be left mostly empty, or include flight time as an orbit parameter.

Astrophysical origin: origin_site from the classification (GC, disk, outer rim). The paper classifies with flag F. So we can include origin_classification: e.g., "disk runaway" or "GC candidate". The ejection velocity is given. Travel time is flight time. Hypothesis_metrics maybe the bound probabilities, but those are in bound assessment. Origin metrics might be the plane-crossing radius r_p. So we can include r_p as a quantity in origin.

Now source_refs: We'll need to point to the paper lines for each candidate's unbound claim. Also for values, we'll use ECSV cells with line numbers.

Let's map candidates to paper numbers:

Candidate numbers from vejection table:

#1 S5-HVS1
#2 B537 (not included)
#3 B576 (not included)
#4 B598 (not included)
#5 HVS5
#6 HVS6
#7 B481
#8 LAMOST HVS1
#9 B434 (not included)
#10 LAMOST HVS2
#11 PG1610+062 (not included)
#12 HVS7
#13 LAMOST HVS4
#14 J0136+2425
#15 B733 (not included)
#16 B711 (not included)
#17 HVS17 (not included; HVS17 is #17, not HVS7)
#18 HVS8
... So HVS7 is #12, HVS8 is #18. Good.

Now for each of those 10, we fill:

record_id: "2101.10878:cand-001" for S5-HVS1, etc. I'll map as:

cand-001: S5-HVS1 (#1)
cand-002: B481 (#7)
cand-003: HVS5 (#5)
cand-004: HVS6 (#6)
cand-005: LAMOST HVS1 (#8)
cand-006: LAMOST HVS2 (#10)
cand-007: HVS7 (#12)
cand-008: LAMOST HVS4 (#13)
cand-009: J0136+2425 (#14)
cand-010: HVS8 (#18)

But the ordering in vejection table is by ejection velocity. I'll simply order them by their table number, but we need consistent record_id numbering, and I'll just assign sequential. So cand-001 = S5-HVS1. Then cand-002 = HVS5? No, I want to include B481 before HVS5 because B481 is #7 and HVS5 is #5, but the paper's order in table is not alphabetical. I'll just use the order of appearance of the candidate names in the text discussion, which is roughly the order they are listed? It doesn't matter as long as it's stable. I'll use the order they appear in the candidate list of possibly unbound: S5-HVS1 (1), HVS5 (5), HVS6 (6), LAMOST HVS1 (8), LAMOST HVS2 (10), LAMOST HVS4 (13), J0136+2425 (14), B481 (7), HVS7 (12), HVS8 (18). So I'll assign cand-001 to S5-HVS1, cand-002 to HVS5, cand-003 to HVS6, cand-004 to LAMOST HVS1, cand-005 to LAMOST HVS2, cand-006 to LAMOST HVS4, cand-007 to J0136+2425, cand-008 to B481, cand-009 to HVS7, cand-010 to HVS8. That's fine.

Now for each, I'll extract from ECSV files:

vejection table: for each number, col_003 (v_ej), col_004 (r_p), col_005 (M), col_006 (F). We'll use those for ejection velocity, plane-crossing radius, stellar mass, and flag.

kinematics_AS: row number matching #. Need to extract: x,y,z, r, v_x,v_y,v_z, v_Grf, v_Grf-v_esc, P_b, x_p,y_p,z_p,r_p, v_x_p,v_y_p,v_z_p, v_Grf_p, v_ej, tau_flight. But careful: the table has two lines per star: the value line and the uncertainty line. The ecsv extraction has two consecutive rows per star: the first row is the value row, second row is the uncertainty row with errors. So for #1, rows 30-31 are value and uncertainty. The ecsv line numbers: col_001 has number 1 at line 30. Then the next line 31 is the uncertainty line starting with "". So we need to extract the value from the value row line 30, and errors from line 31. Similarly for all. In ecsv, the value row has the cleaned numbers (with uncertainties in a separate row). So we can read the value row for each field. For example, for #1 (S5-HVS1), line 30: col_002 = -4.12, col_003=-1.78, col_004=-7.25, col_005=8.52, col_006 empty, col_007=-720, col_008=-353, col_009=-1482, col_010=1680, col_011=1080, col_012=0, col_013=-0.6, col_014=-0.06, col_015=0.0, col_016=0.19, col_017 empty, col_018=-790, col_019=-364, col_020=-1540, col_021=1760, col_022=1810, col_023=4.72. And the next line 31 has the uncertainties as strings: e.g., col_002 = "^+0.17_-0.15". So we can parse the value and the error upper/lower from that.

For the purpose of filling QuantityRecord, we need raw_value from the ECSV cell (the exact string), and cleaned value (plain number). For the error, we can extract from the uncertainty row if available, but the skill says to put uncertainty into error or lower_error/upper_error fields. However, the kinematics table provides asymmetrical errors. For each value, we have a separate error row. So we'll need to fill lower_error and upper_error for each field. For the value row, we'll use raw_value from that cell, value as the plain number. For error fields, we'll source the uncertainty row, line 31, and extract lower and upper bounds. For example, for x, raw_value from value row line 30 col_002 = "-4.12", value = "-4.12" (as string). lower_error from line 31 col_002: "^+0.17_-0.15", which gives lower error 0.15, upper error 0.17. So we'll need to parse that. But the ecsv cell content is exactly that string. So we can include that. We'll set lower_error as the absolute value of the lower error part (0.15), upper_error as 0.17, and error empty. The unit for x is kpc. The column header indicates x unit (kpc) from line 29: " (kpc)". So we can fill unit accordingly.

Similarly for velocity columns: unit (km s-1) from line 29: "(km s -1 )". For probability P_b unit (%) from line? line 29: "(%)" in parentheses for P_b. So we'll use that.

The skill says to use source_refs with ecsv_cell referencing the exact line, column, column_header, raw_value.

Now for the vejection table, we have v_ej, r_p, M, flag. v_ej unit km/s, r_p kpc, M M_sun.

Now for spectrophotometry table, we have distance d (kpc), radial velocity v_rad (km/s). For v_rad, the column has no unit symbol; the header says "v_rad" and unit implied km/s from header. So we'll set unit "km s-1".

The spectrophotometry table row numbers correspond to the # column. So for #1, row 31 (line 31 in ecsv) is the data row. So we can extract from that.

Now the method chain: I'll define steps:

step-01: input_catalog - Gaia EDR3 proper motions, spectrophotometric distances, radial velocities from multiple instruments.
step-02: sample_selection - selection of 30 program stars based on ejection velocity and disk origin criteria.
step-03: distance_estimation - spectrophotometric distances from spectral analysis (or literature).
